content.body:
CONDUCTORS (e.g. copper): conduct electricity easily — many free electrons.
INSULATORS (e.g. rubber): do not conduct — electrons tightly bound.
SEMICONDUCTORS (e.g. silicon, germanium): conduct partially — conductivity increases with temperature or doping.
INTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTORS: Pure silicon (Si). At room temperature, a few electrons gain enough energy to become free, leaving positive 'holes'. Current carried by electrons AND holes.

DOPING: Adding tiny amounts of impurity to increase conductivity.
• n-type: doped with pentavalent atoms (e.g. phosphorus, 5 outer electrons) → extra free electrons (negative carriers)
• p-type: doped with trivalent atoms (e.g. boron, 3 outer electrons) → holes (positive carriers)

p-n JUNCTION DIODE: When p-type and n-type joined.
Forward bias (+ to p, − to n): low resistance, current flows.
Reverse bias (+ to n, − to p): high resistance, very little current.
Application: Rectification (AC → DC), LEDs, solar cells.

TRANSISTOR (NPN or PNP): Three layers — emitter, base, collector.
Small base current controls large collector current → AMPLIFICATION.
Base-emitter junction forward biased, base-collector reverse biased.

questions.question: Semiconductors differ from conductors because:
options: They have fewer free electrons and conductivity increases with temperature
questions.answer: They have fewer free electrons and conductivity increases with temperature
questions.explanation: Unlike metals (conductivity decreases with temperature), semiconductor conductivity increases as more electrons are thermally excited.

questions.question: n-type semiconductors are created by doping with atoms that have:
questions.explanation: Pentavalent dopants (P, As) have 5 valence electrons — 4 form bonds with Si, 1 becomes a free electron → n-type.

questions.question: In p-type semiconductor, majority charge carriers are:
questions.explanation: Trivalent dopants create 'holes' (missing electrons). Holes are the majority carriers in p-type material.

questions.question: A diode in forward bias:
questions.explanation: Forward bias (+ to anode/p-side) overcomes the depletion layer barrier → diode conducts with low resistance.

questions.question: Rectification is the process of:
options: Converting AC to DC using diodes
questions.answer: Converting AC to DC using diodes
questions.explanation: Diodes only conduct in forward direction — blocking reverse half-cycles of AC, converting it to pulsating DC.

questions.question: An LED (Light Emitting Diode) emits light when:
options: In forward bias — electrons and holes recombine releasing photons
questions.answer: In forward bias — electrons and holes recombine releasing photons
questions.explanation: In forward-biased LED, electrons cross junction and recombine with holes, releasing energy as photons (light).

questions.question: In an NPN transistor, the three regions are:
questions.explanation: NPN transistor: n-type emitter, thin p-type base, n-type collector. Electrons flow from emitter to collector.

questions.question: A transistor acts as an amplifier because:
options: Small base current controls large collector current
questions.answer: Small base current controls large collector current
questions.explanation: Current gain (hFE or β) = Ic/Ib. Typical β = 100-400. Small base signal controls much larger collector current.

questions.question: The depletion layer in a p-n junction is:
options: A region depleted of mobile charge carriers at the junction
questions.answer: A region depleted of mobile charge carriers at the junction
questions.explanation: Electrons and holes recombine at junction → region with no free carriers → acts as insulating barrier.

questions.question: Solar cells convert:
questions.explanation: Solar cells use the photovoltaic effect — photons knock electrons free in semiconductor junction, creating EMF.

questions.question: Doping silicon with boron (3 outer electrons) creates:
questions.explanation: Boron has 3 valence electrons — one bond with Si is incomplete → creates a 'hole' → p-type material.

questions.question: In reverse bias, a diode has:
options: High resistance and almost no current flows
questions.answer: High resistance and almost no current flows
questions.explanation: Reverse bias widens the depletion layer → very high resistance → essentially no current (except tiny leakage current).

questions.question: The most commonly used semiconductor material in electronics is:
questions.explanation: Silicon is most widely used — abundant, well-understood, forms stable oxide layer (SiO₂) for chip manufacturing.

questions.question: Transistors replaced vacuum tubes (valves) because they are:
options: Smaller, faster, cheaper, more reliable, use less power
questions.answer: Smaller, faster, cheaper, more reliable, use less power
questions.explanation: Solid-state transistors revolutionised electronics — enabling miniaturisation (microchips) that vacuum tubes couldn't achieve.

questions.question: A full-wave rectifier uses how many diodes?
questions.explanation: A bridge rectifier (full-wave) uses 4 diodes arranged in a bridge circuit — converts both halves of AC cycle to DC.

questions.question: The threshold forward voltage for silicon diode to conduct is approximately:
questions.explanation: Silicon diodes need about 0.6-0.7V forward voltage to overcome the depletion barrier. Germanium needs ~0.2V.

questions.question: In a transistor used as a switch, the two states are:
questions.explanation: Digital logic uses transistors as switches: saturation = switch closed (logic 1), cut-off = switch open (logic 0).

questions.question: The current gain β of a transistor = Ic/Ib. If β = 200 and Ib = 50μA, Ic =
Thermistors are semiconductor devices whose resistance:
questions.explanation: NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistors: higher temp → more electron-hole pairs → lower resistance. Used in temperature sensors.

questions.question: Integrated circuits (ICs) contain millions of:
options: Tiny transistors and other components on a single silicon chip
questions.answer: Tiny transistors and other components on a single silicon chip
questions.explanation: Modern processors contain billions of transistors etched onto silicon chip just millimetres across — the foundation of all modern electronics.
